title: Contradictory Conflict Type
content: Two requirements that cannot both be true simultaneously. Example: auto-save every 30s vs never save without user action. Resolution: find underlying interest; create conditional logic.

title: Resource Conflict Type
content: Conflict arising from limited budget or time for competing needs. Example: Dept A and B both want implementation priority. Resolution: use prioritization framework MoSCoW or WSJF.

title: Stakeholder Conflict Type
content: Different opinions between stakeholders on the same requirement. Example: Marketing says X but Engineering says it is infeasible. Resolution: facilitate dialogue and find compromise.

title: Priority Conflict Type
content: Everyone marks their requirement as Must Have or highest priority. Resolution: apply objective criteria such as MoSCoW or WSJF to rank objectively.

title: Scope Creep Conflict Type
content: Continuous additions beyond the agreed scope. Example: just one more small feature requests. Resolution: return to original scope agreement and defer additions to backlog.

title: Interpretation Conflict Type
content: Different understanding of the same requirement text. Example: real-time means 1 second to user but 5 minutes to developer. Resolution: define precisely with examples and measurable criteria.

title: Harvard Principle 1 - Separate People From Problem
content: Be soft on people but hard on the problem. Use I-statements not You-statements. Example: say I am concerned about the timeline instead of You set an impossible deadline. Reduces emotional defensiveness.
tags: negotiation harvard principle people problem conflict

title: Harvard Principle 2 - Focus on Interests Not Positions
content: Position is what they say they want; Interest is the underlying need why they want it. Example position: report in 5 seconds. Underlying interest: users abandon if slow. Alternative solutions may satisfy the interest.

title: Harvard Principle 3 - Generate Options for Mutual Gain
content: Before deciding brainstorm multiple options using what-if questions; look for trades; expand the pie by finding new resources; phase implementation. Goal is WIN-WIN not WIN-LOSE.
tags: negotiation harvard options win-win brainstorm conflict

title: Harvard Principle 4 - Use Objective Criteria
content: Base decisions on standards not power or pressure. Objective criteria include industry benchmarks; market rates; expert opinions; legal requirements; scientific data; historical precedent; cost-benefit analysis.
tags: negotiation harvard objective criteria standards conflict

title: BATNA - Best Alternative To Negotiated Agreement
content: BATNA is what you will do if no agreement is reached. Your BATNA options: escalate to sponsor; defer feature; implement minimal version; engage mediator. The party with the better BATNA has more power. Always improve your BATNA before negotiating.

title: Reservation Price - Walk-Away Point
content: Reservation price is the minimum acceptable outcome below which you walk away and escalate. Example: goal is 3-second response time; reservation price is 10 seconds maximum. Know your walk-away point before entering negotiation.

title: ZOPA - Zone of Possible Agreement
content: ZOPA is the overlap between both parties reservation prices where agreement is possible. If ZOPA exists agreement can be reached. If no ZOPA exists you need to change the game or escalate to decision maker.

title: BATNA Preparation Worksheet
content: Worksheet elements: Best Alternative if No Deal; Reservation Price; Target/Aspiration; Key Interests; Constraints; Potential Trades. Complete for both your side and estimated other side before negotiating.

title: Conflict Resolution Step 1 - Identify and Acknowledge
content: Recognize conflict early - do not ignore. Document disagreement clearly. Identify all parties. Acknowledge each party concerns. Create psychological safety by saying your concerns are valid. Early identification prevents escalation.

title: Conflict Resolution Step 2 - Analyze Root Cause
content: Separate positions from interests. Understand emotional drivers such as fear frustration ego. Assess impact of each option. Identify constraints and dependencies. Map stakeholder power and interests. Root cause analysis before proposing solutions.

title: Conflict Resolution Step 3 - Generate Options
content: Brainstorm alternatives without judging. Look for win-win solutions. Consider phased approaches. Explore trades and compromises. Evaluate trade-offs objectively. Generate multiple options before evaluating any single one.

title: Conflict Resolution Step 4 - Negotiate and Facilitate
content: Facilitate discussion between parties. Present facts objectively as BA stays neutral. Use objective criteria to evaluate options. Focus on interests not positions. Apply BATNA and ZOPA analysis. BA role is facilitator not advocate.

title: Conflict Resolution Step 5 - Escalate If Needed
content: Escalate to appropriate decision maker. Provide recommendation with clear rationale. Present options not just the problem. Accept and implement final decision. Do not take sides and let authority decide. Escalation is a valid resolution tool.

title: Conflict Resolution Step 6 - Document and Communicate
content: Record the resolution and reasoning. Update affected requirements. Communicate outcome to all stakeholders. Log decision in Decision Log for future reference. Monitor for residual issues after resolution.

title: Escalation Matrix - When and To Whom
content: Technical feasibility dispute escalates to Tech Lead or Architect. Budget conflict escalates to PM or PMO. Business priority conflict escalates to Sponsor or Product Owner. Cross-department conflict escalates to Steering Committee. Legal issues escalate to Legal or Compliance.

title: Escalation Memorandum Template
content: Escalation memo sections: TO decision maker; FROM BA; DATE; RE brief description; Executive Summary 2-3 sentences; Background context; The Conflict with positions and interests of each party; Options Considered with pros cons and impact; Recommendation with rationale; Decision Needed By date.

title: Decision Log Template
content: Decision log columns: ID; Date; Issue; Parties; Options Considered; Decision; Rationale; Decided By. Example entry: D-001 Report response time conflict between User and Tech resolved as 10sec with progress bar by Product Owner.

title: Conflict Resolution Checklist - Preparation
content: Preparation items: conflict documented clearly; all parties identified; interests not just positions understood for each party; BATNA identified for yourself and others; ZOPA estimated; objective criteria gathered.

title: Conflict Resolution Checklist - During Negotiation
content: During negotiation items: separated people from problem; focused on interests not positions; generated multiple options; used objective criteria; remained neutral as BA facilitator not advocate; documented discussion points.

title: Conflict Resolution Checklist - Post Resolution
content: Resolution items: decision reached or escalated appropriately; decision documented with rationale; requirements updated to reflect decision; all parties informed; decision logged for future reference; relationship maintained - no burned bridges.

title: Influence Without Authority - Expertise Power
content: BA typically has no formal authority. Expertise power: be the most prepared person in the room; know the domain deeply; provide insights others cannot; reference standards and benchmarks to establish credibility.

title: Influence Without Authority - Relationship Power
content: Relationship power: build genuine rapport before you need something; help others succeed as reciprocity works; remember personal details; be reliable and trustworthy. Reciprocity: helped them last month so they are willing to compromise now.

title: Influence Without Authority - Process Power
content: Process power: control the agenda as the facilitator shapes outcomes; establish the framework for decision-making; document the official version; define the criteria for evaluation. The person who controls process influences outcomes.

title: Cialdini 6 Influence Principles for BA
content: Six principles: Reciprocity give before you ask; Commitment start small build up; Social Proof others are doing it; Authority experts recommend cite IEEE BABOK; Liking build rapport find common ground; Scarcity limited time opportunity highlight deadline constraints.

title: Interest Discovery Questions Framework
content: WHY questions: why is this important to you; what problem are you solving. WHAT questions: what does success look like; what are your constraints. HOW questions: how flexible is this requirement. REFRAMING: what if we approached this differently; could we achieve same goal by different means.

title: Difficult Conversation Scripts
content: Scripts for: stakeholder insisting on unrealistic requirement - explore underlying need; two stakeholders disagreeing - summarize both interests; stakeholder saying no negotiation - ask for reasoning to find alternatives; scope creep request - capture it then return to agreed scope.

title: Why Requirements Conflict Is Inevitable
content: Four root causes of conflict: Different Perspectives same goal different priorities; Scarcity limited budget time people; Ambiguity different interpretations of same text; Organizational Politics power dynamics turf wars hidden agendas. Healthy conflict improves requirements; unhealthy conflict delays projects.

title: Influence Without Authority - Coalition Power
content: Coalition power: build alliances with key stakeholders; find sponsors for your recommendations; pre-align with decision makers before meetings. Example: I have already discussed this with the product owner and CFO so they support this approach.

title: Influence Without Authority - Information Power
content: Information power: be the hub of project knowledge; connect people who should talk to each other; share relevant information proactively. Example: let me connect you with the finance team who has this data you need.
